Keywords: backend, remote, s3, gcs, azurerm, state
Description: Use remote backend for team collaboration and state locking
Do: Configure S3/GCS/Azure Blob backend with state locking
Don't: Use local backend for team projects or production
Code Example Good: terraform { backend "s3" { bucket = "my-tf-state" key = "prod/terraform.tfstate" region = "us-east-1" encrypt = true dynamodb_table = "tf-locks" } }

Do: Enable encryption on backend storage (S3 SSE, GCS encryption)
Code Example Good: terraform { backend "s3" { bucket = "my-tf-state" key = "terraform.tfstate" encrypt = true kms_key_id = "arn:aws:kms:us-east-1:123456789:key/xxx" } }
Code Example Bad: terraform { backend "s3" { bucket = "my-tf-state" key = "terraform.tfstate" # No encryption } }

Description: Enable state locking to prevent concurrent modifications
Do: Configure DynamoDB table for S3 or native locking for GCS/Azure
Don't: Run terraform apply without state locking
Code Example Good: terraform { backend "s3" { dynamodb_table = "terraform-locks" } } resource "aws_dynamodb_table" "locks" { name = "terraform-locks" billing_mode = "PAY_PER_REQUEST" hash_key = "LockID" attribute { name = "LockID" type = "S" } }
Code Example Bad: terraform { backend "s3" { bucket = "my-state" # No dynamodb_table - no locking } }

Do: Use different state files/paths for dev/staging/prod
Don't: Share single state file across environments
Code Example Good: # Option 1: Different keys terraform { backend "s3" { key = "${var.environment}/terraform.tfstate" } } # Option 2: Workspaces terraform workspace new prod terraform workspace new staging
Code Example Bad: terraform { backend "s3" { key = "terraform.tfstate" # Same state for all envs } }

Do: Use terraform workspace for managing multiple environments
Code Example Good: locals { env = terraform.workspace } resource "aws_instance" "web" { instance_type = local.env == "prod" ? "t3.large" : "t3.micro" tags = { Environment = local.env } }
Code Example Bad: resource "aws_instance" "web" { instance_type = "t3.large" # Hardcoded, same for all envs }

Description: Never commit state files to version control
Code Example Bad: # Bad: Committing state to git git add terraform.tfstate git commit -m "Update state"

Description: Restrict access to state files using IAM policies
Do: Use IAM policies to limit who can read/write state
Don't: Allow broad access to state bucket
Code Example Good: resource "aws_s3_bucket_policy" "state" { bucket = aws_s3_bucket.state.id policy = jsonencode({ Statement = [{ Effect = "Allow" Principal = { AWS = "arn:aws:iam::123456789:role/terraform" } Action = ["s3:GetObject", "s3:PutObject"] Resource = "${aws_s3_bucket.state.arn}/*" }] }) }
Code Example Bad: resource "aws_s3_bucket" "state" { bucket = "tf-state" } # Public or overly permissive access

Description: Enable versioning on state bucket for recovery
Do: Enable S3 versioning to recover from corrupted state
Code Example Bad: resource "aws_s3_bucket" "state" { bucket = "tf-state" } # No versioning - state loss risk

Description: Plan state migrations carefully with backup
Do: Backup state, use terraform state mv for refactoring
Don't: Move resources without planning or backup
Code Example Good: # Before migration terraform state pull > backup.tfstate # Move resource terraform state mv aws_instance.old aws_instance.new # Verify terraform plan
Code Example Bad: # Bad: Moving without backup terraform state mv aws_instance.old aws_instance.new # Oops, wrong move, no way back

Description: Use import blocks for importing existing resources
Do: Use import blocks (TF 1.5+) for declarative imports
Don't: Use terraform import command without planning
Code Example Good: # Terraform 1.5+ import block import { to = aws_instance.web id = "i-1234567890abcdef0" } resource "aws_instance" "web" { # ... configuration } # Then run: terraform plan
Code Example Bad: # Imperative import - harder to track terraform import aws_instance.web i-1234567890abcdef0

Description: Mark sensitive outputs to prevent exposure
Do: Use sensitive = true for outputs containing secrets
Code Example Bad: output "db_password" { value = aws_db_instance.main.password # Exposed in logs }

Description: Control state refresh for large infrastructures
Do: Use -refresh=false when refresh is not needed
Code Example Good: # Skip refresh when not needed terraform plan -refresh=false # Target specific resources terraform plan -target=aws_instance.web
Code Example Bad: # Slow: Always refreshing 1000+ resources terraform plan # Takes 10+ minutes

Do: Use -target for debugging or specific updates
Code Example Good: # Debug specific resource terraform plan -target=aws_instance.web # Apply specific change terraform apply -target=aws_instance.web
Code Example Bad: # Bad: Using target to avoid fixing dependencies terraform apply -target=aws_instance.web # Regularly

Description: Consider Terraform Cloud for team features
Do: Use Terraform Cloud for policy, cost estimation, private registry
Don't: Build custom CI/CD when TF Cloud provides features
Code Example Bad: # Building custom CI/CD pipeline # When TF Cloud provides these features OOB

Description: Use data sources to share state between configurations
Do: Use terraform_remote_state or data sources for cross-config data
Code Example Good: data "terraform_remote_state" "vpc" { backend = "s3" config = { bucket = "tf-state" key = "vpc/terraform.tfstate" region = "us-east-1" } } resource "aws_instance" "web" { subnet_id = data.terraform_remote_state.vpc.outputs.subnet_id }
Code Example Bad: resource "aws_instance" "web" { subnet_id = "subnet-12345678" # Hardcoded from another config }
